explanations.1: An amphiarthrosis is a slightly mobile joint, such as the intervertebral discs that unite adjacent vertebrae. Diarthroses are freely moveable, while synarthroses are immobile.
explanations.2: The manubriosternal joint is a cartilaginous joint that is immobile (synarthrosis) to protect the heart. The pubic symphysis and intervertebral discs are amphiarthroses, and the shoulder is a diarthrosis.
explanations.3: The metacarpophalangeal joint is biaxial, allowing for two planes of movement. The elbow is uniaxial, the hip is multiaxial, and the pubic symphysis is an amphiarthrosis.
explanations.4: By definition, synovial joints feature a fluid-filled joint cavity where bones articulate. They are always functionally classified as diarthroses, not synarthroses or amphiarthroses.
explanations.5: A syndesmosis is a fibrous joint where parallel bones, like the tibia and fibula, are united by connective tissue. Symphyses are cartilaginous, and sutures/gomphoses are different types of fibrous joints.
explanations.6: The interosseous membrane of the forearm is a wide sheet of fibrous connective tissue forming a syndesmosis. Gomphoses and sutures are narrow joints, and a synostosis is a fused bone.
explanations.7: A gomphosis is a specialized fibrous joint (peg-and-socket) that anchors a tooth to its bony socket in the jaw. It does not contain a joint cavity or connect parallel leg bones.
explanations.8: A syndesmosis is a fibrous joint where bones are joined by a ligament or interosseous membrane. Sutures unite skull bones and gomphoses anchor teeth.
explanations.9: A symphysis is a cartilaginous joint specifically united by fibrocartilage. Cartilaginous joints lack a joint cavity, which is the defining feature of synovial joints.
explanations.10: A synchondrosis is a cartilaginous joint joined by hyaline cartilage, such as the first sternocostal joint. The pubic symphysis is a symphysis, not a synchondrosis.
explanations.11: Adjacent vertebrae are joined by intervertebral discs made of fibrocartilage, forming a symphysis. The first rib/sternum is a synchondrosis, and the radius/ulna is a syndesmosis.
explanations.12: The epiphyseal plate is a temporary joint where the bone segments are united by hyaline cartilage, classifying it as a synchondrosis. A synostosis occurs only after the plate ossifies.
explanations.13: Ball-and-socket joints are multiaxial, providing the greatest range of motion in all three planes. Hinge, condyloid, and plane joints offer fewer axes of movement.
explanations.14: A hinge joint, like the elbow, is uniaxial because it only allows for movement in one plane. Saddle and condyloid joints are biaxial, and ball-and-socket joints are multiaxial.
explanations.15: A plane joint is a type of synovial joint where bones slide against each other. Synostosis, sutures, and synchondroses are non-synovial joints (fibrous, cartilaginous, or fused).
explanations.16: Synovial joints contain a joint cavity filled with synovial fluid for lubrication. The bones are not directly connected by tissue but are enclosed in an articular capsule.
explanations.17: The synovial membrane is the inner layer of the articular capsule that secretes synovial fluid into the joint cavity. Articular cartilage, not the membrane, covers the bone surfaces.
explanations.18: The radiocarpal joint (wrist) is a condyloid joint, which is a biaxial diarthrosis. The proximal radioulnar joint is a pivot joint, not a condyloid joint.
explanations.19: A meniscus is a C-shaped fibrocartilage pad that provides padding and shock absorption between articulating bones. A bursa is the fluid-filled sac mentioned in option B.
explanations.20: The plane joints between the articular processes of vertebrae allow for gliding movements that contribute to lateral flexion of the spine. Dorsiflexion and abduction are limb movements.
explanations.21: Eversion is the movement of the foot that turns the sole away from the midline. Inversion turns the sole toward the midline, and dorsiflexion lifts the top of the foot.
explanations.22: Circumduction is the circular movement of a body region that combines flexion, extension, abduction, and adduction. Rotation occurs around a single long axis.
explanations.23: Supination is the forearm rotation that turns the palm to the forward (anatomical) position. Pronation turns the palm backward.
explanations.24: Abduction is the movement that takes a limb laterally away from the midline of the body. Adduction brings the limb toward the midline.
explanations.25: The rotator cuff muscles provide the primary structural support for the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint. Ligaments and the glenoid labrum provide additional but secondary stability.
explanations.26: The annular ligament encircles the head of the radius, supporting it at the proximal radioulnar pivot joint. This allows for rotation during pronation and supination.
explanations.27: The medial meniscus is physically attached to the tibial collateral ligament (MCL). The ACL and PCL are intracapsular ligaments that resist anterior/posterior displacement.
explanations.28: The ankle (talocrural) joint is a uniaxial hinge joint allowing for dorsiflexion and plantar flexion. Inversion and eversion occur at the subtalar joint, not the talocrural joint.
explanations.29: The orientation of the articular processes in the thoracic region allows for the greatest range of axial rotation in the vertebral column. Lumbar vertebrae largely prohibit rotation.
explanations.30: Intramembranous ossification is the process that forms the flat bones of the skull, such as the top and sides. Limb bones are formed via endochondral ossification.
explanations.31: Synovial joints develop from the joint interzone, a region of mesenchyme between developing bone models. This process eventually creates the joint cavity.
explanations.32: Endochondral ossification is the developmental process where a hyaline cartilage model is replaced by bone tissue. It is responsible for forming most of the skeleton.
